name: Afghanistan (Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan / د افغانستان اسلامي امارت)
summary: Afghanistan is a South/Central Asian religious-law state operating under a Hanafi-Sunni Sharia framework. Following the August 2021 Taliban takeover, the prior 2004 Constitution and Civil Code 1977 framework were effectively suspended; the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan governs by Hanafi Sharia interpretation under Taliban-issued decrees. The 1977 Civil Code (Qanun-e Madani) drawing on Hanafi jurisprudence with Egyptian civil-code influence formally governs civil matters where not displaced by Taliban decrees. Custody (hadana) and guardianship (wilaya) are governed by Civil Code arts. 234-256 within the residual framework. The Supreme Court framework was restructured post-2021; the Supreme Court of the Islamic Emirate operates under Hanafi Sharia. Family-law matters are heard at first instance in Primary Courts. Psychology profession regulation is uncertain post-2021. Afghanistan is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under Hanafi Sharia welfare-of-the-child framework. Afghanistan is non-Hague Convention.

statutory_framework.title: Civil Code — Custody and guardianship
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal Civil Code drawing on Hanafi jurisprudence with Egyptian civil-code influence. Arts. 234-256 govern hadana (custody) and wilaya (guardianship) within residual framework where not displaced by post-2021 Taliban decrees.

statutory_framework.title: Taliban decrees on women, family, marriage
statutory_framework.relevance: Series of decrees by the Islamic Emirate governing women's rights, family-law, marriage, divorce, and child custody under strict Hanafi Sharia interpretation.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of the Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan
apex_courts.role: Apex court for civil and criminal matters operating under Hanafi Sharia interpretation.

professional_regulators.name: Ministry of Public Health, Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan
professional_regulators.role: Federal regulator of health professionals; clinical psychology regulation uncertain post-2021.

anonymisation_convention: Afghan family-court decisions are anonymised per court practice using initials where published.

key_developments.title: Constitution of 1923 (King Amanullah Khan) + first modernisation
key_developments.description: First Constitution of Afghanistan promulgated by King Amanullah Khan 1923 — landmark legal-modernisation effort attempting Western-influenced reforms in family-law including marriage-registration, abolition of bride-price, and minimum-marriage-age. Reform subsequently substantially reversed under successor administrations. Foundational moment for Afghan codification trajectory.

key_developments.title: Civil Code 1977 (Qanun-e Madani)
key_developments.description: Federal Civil Code enacted 1977 (under President Mohammed Daoud Khan administration) drawing on Hanafi jurisprudence with significant Egyptian civil-code influence (Sanhuri-tradition transplant). Arts. 234-256 govern hadana (custody) and wilaya (guardianship). Substantively retained as residual framework where not displaced by post-2021 Taliban decrees.

key_developments.title: Afghanistan ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
key_developments.description: Afghanistan ratified the UNCRC on 28 March 1994 — framing the (then-Islamic Republic) family-law-reform trajectory toward best-interest-of-the-child substantive doctrine. Post-2021-Taliban-takeover UN-CRC engagement substantially constrained but treaty-ratification formally remains operative.

key_developments.title: First Taliban regime (1996-2001) + Hanafi-Deobandi-Sharia governance
key_developments.description: First Taliban regime governed 1996-2001 under strict Hanafi-Deobandi Sharia interpretation — substantially suspended civil-law framework operating under decree-based governance. Historical precedent for the post-2021 Taliban governance framework.

key_developments.title: Constitution 2004 (Islamic Republic) + Hanafi-Sharia-as-primary-source
key_developments.description: Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Afghanistan adopted 26 January 2004 under interim Karzai administration following 2001 Bonn Agreement. Art. 3 declares no law shall contravene tenets and provisions of Islam. Art. 130 references Hanafi-jurisprudence-as-default-rule-of-decision where no statutory provision applies. Foundational constitutional framework for the Islamic Republic 2004-2021.

key_developments.title: Elimination of Violence Against Women Law (EVAW)
key_developments.description: Federal Elimination of Violence Against Women Law enacted 19 July 2009 by Presidential decree under Karzai administration — establishing protection mechanisms, mandatory-reporting obligations, and explicit recognition of psychological violence within the family unit. Subsequently substantially suspended post-2021 under Taliban governance.

key_developments.title: Shia Personal Status Law (Twelver Shia) + multi-community framework consolidation
key_developments.description: Shia Personal Status Law enacted 2009 (with controversial elements subsequently revised) — providing Twelver-Shia community framework for Hazara and other Shia Afghan communities. Operating parallel to Hanafi-Sunni framework of Civil Code 1977 for the multi-community personal-status framework during the Islamic Republic era 2004-2021.

key_developments.title: Taliban takeover August 2021 + Islamic Emirate restoration + framework suspension
key_developments.description: Taliban takeover 15 August 2021 ending Islamic Republic era. Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan re-established. 2004 Constitution substantively suspended; Elimination of Violence Against Women Law 2009 effectively suspended; governance by Taliban decrees under strict Hanafi-Deobandi Sharia interpretation. Subsequent decrees substantially constraining women's rights including in family-law domain.

key_developments.title: Taliban decrees on women + family-law domain + girls' education suspension
key_developments.description: Series of Taliban decrees throughout 2022-2024 substantially affecting family-law domain: girls' education suspension above grade 6 (March 2022), women's-employment restrictions, family-court hearings predominantly conducted by male judges with restricted women's access, child-custody framework operating under decree-based Hanafi-Deobandi interpretation. Substantial impact on women's family-law access without formal codification reset.

key_developments.title: Vice and Virtue Ministry framework + women's-rights decrees + UNAMA institutional engagement
key_developments.description: Ministry for the Propagation of Virtue and Prevention of Vice (Amr bil Ma'ruf wa Nahi anil Munkar) operational framework substantively distinctive globally — substantively significant decree-based regulatory framework affecting family-law adjudication context. UN Assistance Mission in Afghanistan (UNAMA) institutional engagement under constrained-access framework. Substantively significant Hanafi-Deobandi institutional context affecting Supreme Court of the Islamic Emirate jurisdictional framework.

key_developments.title: Supreme Court of the Islamic Emirate — Hanafi-Deobandi-decree-based welfare-of-the-child substantive register
key_developments.description: Supreme Court of the Islamic Emirate continues to operate under Hanafi-Deobandi-Sharia interpretation with decree-based custody jurisprudence framework. Substantive welfare-of-the-child analysis under Hanafi-jurisprudence-bounded decree-framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label. Family-law-implementation substantially constrained for women litigants; UN-CRC and international children's-rights-monitoring engagement substantially constrained post-2021.

structural_findings: Afghanistan operates a Hanafi-Sunni-Deobandi Sharia religious-law framework — structurally distinctive within the corpus as a state under unilateral religious-law governance post-2021 with prior 2004-Islamic-Republic constitutional framework substantively suspended.

Post-2021 governance by decree under Hanafi-Deobandi Sharia interpretation is structurally distinctive within the corpus — places Afghanistan within the Shariah-as-supreme-law-decree-based-governance cluster alongside Iran 1979 (Iran maintains formal Constitution), Saudi Arabia (Quran-and-Sunnah-as-Constitution Basic Law 1992), and historically Taliban 1996-2001.

Civil Code 1977 + Egyptian civil-code-influence pattern (Sanhuri-tradition transplant) places Afghanistan in the Egyptian-civil-code-transplant cluster alongside Syria, Iraq, Jordan, Libya, and select Gulf states within the residual framework — distinctive within the religious-law constitutional regime cluster.

Multi-phase legal-system evolution: 1923-Amanullah-modernisation + 1977-Civil-Code-Egyptian-influence + 1996-2001-First-Taliban-regime + 2001-Bonn-Agreement + 2004-Constitution-Islamic-Republic + 2009-EVAW-Law + 2017-Shia-Personal-Status-Law + 2021-Taliban-takeover-Islamic-Emirate-restoration + 2022-2024-decree-based-governance — most discontinuous-codification trajectory within the corpus.

Multi-community personal-status framework during Islamic Republic era 2004-2021: Hanafi-Sunni Civil Code 1977 + Shia Personal Status Law 2009 — operating in parallel; substantially constrained post-2021 under unitary Hanafi-Deobandi Sharia governance.
Non-Hague-1980-Convention status places Afghanistan in the non-Hague-Central-Asian cluster alongside Pakistan (acceded 2017), Iran, and the Central-Asian republics.
UNCRC ratification 1994 formally remains operative but implementation substantially constrained post-2021 — UN-CRC and international children's-rights-monitoring engagement substantially constrained.

Substantial women's family-law-access constraints post-2021 — family-court hearings predominantly conducted by male judges, women's-legal-representation restricted, girls' education suspension above grade 6, women's-employment restrictions affecting family-financial-dynamics — places Afghanistan within the most-constrained-women's-family-law-access cluster within the corpus.

Psychology profession regulation substantially uncertain post-2021 — Ministry of Public Health framework retained but clinical-psychology regulation framework substantially unclear within the constrained operational context.
editorial_notes: Afghanistan jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-08 from 4 to 10 key_developments with full Amanullah-to-contemporary trajectory: 1923-Constitution-of-Amanullah-Khan-first-modernisation + 1977-Civil-Code-Qanun-e-Madani-Egyptian-influence + 1994-UNCRC-ratification + 1996-First-Taliban-regime-1996-2001 + 2004-Constitution-Islamic-Republic + 2009-Elimination-of-Violence-Against-Women-Law-EVAW + 2017-Shia-Personal-Status-Law + 2021-Taliban-takeover-Islamic-Emirate-restoration-framework-suspension + 2022-Taliban-decrees-on-women + 2024-Supreme-Court-Islamic-Emirate-Hanafi-Deobandi-decree-based.

Hanafi-Sunni-Deobandi religious-law (Civil Code 1977 Egyptian-civil-code-influence residual + Taliban decrees post-2021 + non-Hague Convention). Structurally distinctive post-2021 governance + most-discontinuous-codification trajectory within corpus + substantial women's family-law-access constraints.

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive welfare-of-the-child analysis under Hanafi-Deobandi-Sharia decree-based framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins South/Central-Asian + Hanafi-Sunni-Deobandi-religious-law + Shariah-as-supreme-law-decree-based-governance (with Iran, Saudi Arabia, historically Taliban 1996-2001) + Egyptian-civil-code-transplant-Sanhuri (with Syria, Iraq, Jordan, Libya) + post-constitutional-suspension-distinctive + non-Hague-Central-Asian-Convention + most-constrained-women's-family-law-access clusters within the corpus.
